medications.indication_rationale: First-line oral antibiotic for mild, non-purulent lower-limb cellulitis in a systemically-well patient, covering the usual Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes. In telehealth the AI Doctor should arrange for this to be commenced as part of urgent same-day in-person assessment; a prescription is required.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 500 mg orally four times a day
contraindications_in_this_case: Penicillin allergy (none reported by this patient)

medications.drug_name: Topical antibiotic (e.g. topical mupirocin/fusidic acid) as sole treatment
medications.indication_rationale: A topical antibiotic does not treat surrounding cellulitis and, applied to a chronic wound, promotes resistance and contact sensitisation — it is not a substitute for systemic antibiotics here.

medications.drug_name: Compression bandaging / stockings during the acute infection
medications.indication_rationale: Compression is the eventual mainstay for venous ulcer healing, but it must NOT be (re)applied during acute cellulitis or before arterial supply is confirmed by ABPI/pulses. Reinstating tight compression now — on a hot, painful, infected, unassessed limb — risks harm; defer until the acute infection and pain settle and arterial disease is excluded.
medications.deprescribing_note: Advise the patient NOT to reapply her compression stockings until the acute infection and severe pain have settled and a clinician has checked the circulation.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Community/wound-care nursing is needed for ongoing cleansing, an absorbent (e.g. alginate) dressing regimen for the heavy exudate, and re-initiation of compression therapy once the acute infection subsides.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the antibiotic exactly four times a day for the full 7 days, ideally on an empty stomach, and do not stop early even once the wound improves.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Steady antibiotic levels and a complete course are needed to clear the cellulitis and prevent relapse and resistance.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strictly elevate the leg above heart level whenever sitting or lying, and keep the dressing clean and dry between changes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation uses gravity to reduce venous oedema and throbbing pressure, easing pain and aiding healing while the infection is treated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once the acute infection and pain settle and a clinician has confirmed the circulation is adequate, resume regular compression therapy and long-term venous management to prevent recurrence.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Compression is the mainstay of venous ulcer healing and recurrence prevention, but only after infection control and arterial assessment.

patient_education_points.point: The long-standing leg veins struggle to pump blood back, so fluid pools and the skin breaks down into an ulcer; a bacterial infection has now set into that open wound and the surrounding skin, which is why it has become red, hot, more painful and smelly.
The wound needs to be seen in person today to be cleaned and dressed properly and to start antibiotics — this is not something that can be treated over the phone alone.
Venous ulcers are notoriously slow to heal: the pain and smell should ease within 48-72 hours on antibiotics, but the ulcer itself will take many weeks of dressing changes and compression to close.
Do not reapply the tight compression stockings until the acute infection and severe pain have settled and a clinician has checked the leg's circulation.

follow_up_plan.interval: in-person wound assessment and antibiotics today; clinical review in 48 hours to assess the cellulitis response
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response of the surrounding erythema and pain to antibiotics, wound exudate and appearance, and confirmation of adequate arterial supply before compression is restarted
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any spread of the redness up the calf, whole-leg swelling, fever or rigors, or worsening severe pain — seek urgent/emergency care rather than waiting for the 48-hour review

must_include_items: Recognise an acute secondary cellulitis superimposed on a chronic venous leg ulcer
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment for wound cleansing/dressing and to commence oral antibiotics
Advise oral antibiotics (e.g. flucloxacillin) for the cellulitis via a prescriber, given no penicillin allergy
Advise strict leg elevation and keeping the dressing clean and dry
Explicitly advise NOT to reapply compression stockings during the acute infection and before arterial assessment
Give clear ED/urgent safety-netting for spreading erythema, whole-leg swelling, fever/rigors or pain out of proportion
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to an appropriate same-day service (own GP, urgent care clinic, or after-hours/community wound service) rather than a specific named clinic
Cephalexin as an alternative first-line oral antibiotic if flucloxacillin is unsuitable
errors_of_omission: Failing to identify the new cellulitis and treating it as an unchanged chronic wound
Failing to arrange same-day in-person wound assessment and antibiotics
Failing to give explicit deterioration safety-netting
errors_of_commission: Advising the patient to reapply compression stockings during the acute infection or before arterial assessment
Recommending a topical antibiotic alone instead of systemic antibiotics
Attempting to manage the wound entirely remotely without in-person assessment
Under-triaging to routine GP (1-2 weeks) or self-care
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the cellulitis, arranging same-day in-person wound care + oral antibiotics, and giving deterioration safety-netting. Advising compression during acute infection, or managing purely remotely, is a critical error of commission.
